lint.output: ruff check found 8 fixable linting issues

findings.title: Unvalidated email access grants JWT for any gallery — effectively unauthenticated
findings.description: The auth_view handler issues JWT tokens for ANY syntactically valid email without checking if that email is authorized to access the specific gallery. This means an attacker can access any gallery by providing a valid email address. The code explicitly acknowledges this limitation in a TODO comment (CLIENT-03), but it is a critical security issue that should be resolved.
findings.fix_suggestion: Implement email allowlist validation in handle_view_auth() before issuing the JWT. Check against a per-gallery allowlist stored in the gallery item.
findings.evidence: TODO comment at line 252-258 explicitly states the issue
findings.impact: Any attacker can gain unauthorized access to any gallery by submitting any valid email address. This bypasses the intended access control model.

findings.title: Duplicate import of uuid module in auth_view handler
findings.description: The uuid module is imported twice in lambda/auth_view/handler.py (lines 9 and 25). This was flagged by ruff linter as F811.
findings.fix_suggestion: Remove the duplicate import uuid from line 25. Keep the first import at line 9.
findings.evidence: import uuid appears at both line 9 and line 25
findings.impact: Code maintainability is reduced. The duplicate import suggests incomplete code review.

findings.title: Unused imports in test_zip_privacy.py
findings.description: The test file imports BytesIO and MagicMock but never uses them. Detected by ruff F401 rule.
findings.fix_suggestion: Remove unused imports. Run 'ruff check --fix' to fix automatically.
findings.evidence: Unused imports: io.BytesIO and unittest.mock.MagicMock
findings.impact: Test code clarity is reduced.

findings.title: Import ordering violations in 5 test files
findings.description: Five test files have un-sorted or un-formatted import blocks (I001 ruff rule).
findings.fix_suggestion: Run 'ruff check --fix' to automatically organize imports in all test files.
findings.evidence: Import blocks not sorted according to isort conventions
findings.impact: Code style inconsistency. Indicates lack of code quality discipline.

findings.title: Admin handler uses global mutable state for caching secrets with 5-minute TTL
findings.description: The admin handler caches secrets in global variables with a 5-minute TTL. If a secret is rotated, the Lambda will continue using the old cached key for up to 5 minutes.
findings.fix_suggestion: Reduce TTL to 1 minute or implement proactive secret rotation checks.
findings.evidence: _ADMIN_KEY_CACHE_TTL = 300 (5 minutes)
findings.impact: Secret rotation delays could cause authentication failures or security issues.

findings.title: Email validation regex is overly permissive
findings.description: The email validation regex does not properly validate according to RFC 5322.
findings.fix_suggestion: Use a more robust email validation library or improve the regex pattern.
findings.impact: Invalid email addresses could be accepted.

findings.title: Bare except clause in JWT verification
findings.description: The verify_jwt() function uses bare 'except Exception' without specific error handling.
findings.fix_suggestion: Catch specific exceptions (ValueError, JSONDecodeError) and log appropriately.
findings.evidence:
except Exception:
    return None
findings.impact: Harder to debug issues. All exceptions treated the same way.

findings.title: Optimistic update retry logic without backoff delay
findings.description: The _optimistic_update function retries on version conflicts without backoff, which could waste Lambda time under high concurrency.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add exponential backoff with jitter between retries.
findings.evidence: MAX_OPTIMISTIC_RETRIES = 3 with immediate retry
findings.impact: Potential Lambda timeout under high concurrency.

findings.title: Filename sanitization could be more robust for edge cases
findings.description: The _sanitize_filename function prevents directory traversal but doesn't explicitly check for null bytes or control characters.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add explicit validation to reject null bytes and control characters.
findings.evidence: Uses os.path.basename() and regex filtering but no null byte check
findings.impact: Low risk, but edge cases like 'file\x00.jpg' could cause issues.

findings.title: HTML frontend uses innerHTML with template strings — potential XSS
findings.description: The admin.html uses innerHTML in 20 places with template literals. While an esc() function exists and is used in some cases, coverage is not complete.
findings.fix_suggestion: Audit all innerHTML assignments. Use esc() for all user-controlled data or use textContent instead.
findings.evidence: Multiple innerHTML assignments. esc() function defined at line 3671 but not consistently applied.
findings.impact: If user-controlled data contains HTML/JS, it could be executed (stored XSS).

findings.title: ThreadPoolExecutor with large max_workers in Lambda
findings.description: The admin handler uses ThreadPoolExecutor with max_workers=50 and max_workers=20, which could exceed Lambda's thread limits.
findings.fix_suggestion: Reduce max_workers to 5-10 for Lambda environment.
findings.evidence: concurrent.futures.ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=50)
findings.impact: Potential resource exhaustion or GIL contention.

findings.title: Missing error handling for CloudFront signed URL generation
findings.description: The make_signed_url function raises exceptions on failure without graceful fallback.
findings.fix_suggestion: Wrap signing operation in try-except and provide helpful error messages.
findings.evidence: signature = private_key.sign(...) without try-except
findings.impact: Users get generic errors instead of helpful debugging information.

lint.output: ESLint couldn't find an eslint.config.(js|mjs|cjs) file. From ESLint v9.0.0, the default configuration file is now eslint.config.js

tests.output: Test Files: 2 failed | 3 passed (5). Tests: 83 passed (83). Failures: terraform/.terraform/modules/codebuild/tests/products.test.ts and terraform/.terraform/modules/codebuild/tests/verify-dist.test.ts cannot be compiled (missing tsconfig)

findings.title: Missing ESLint configuration file
findings.description: ESLint v9 requires eslint.config.js (or .mjs/.cjs) but the project has none. The CI/CD buildspec-ci.yml expects 'npm run lint' to succeed, but it will fail on every run.
findings.evidence: buildspec-ci.yml lines 12-13 run 'npm run lint', but eslint.config.js does not exist. Running 'npm run lint' produces: ESLint couldn't find an eslint.config.(js|mjs|cjs) file.
findings.impact: CI/CD pipeline will fail on every PR. Linting cannot run, breaking code quality checks.

findings.title: Test suite includes non-JS files that cannot compile
findings.description: Vitest is configured to run tests and discovers TypeScript files in terraform/.terraform/modules/codebuild/tests/ which have no tsconfig.json. This causes 2 test suites to fail with TSCONFIG_ERROR during 'npm test'.
findings.fix_suggestion: Configure vitest to exclude terraform module tests. Add to vitest config or package.json: exclude: ['terraform/**']. Alternatively, move terraform tests to a separate test suite or add tsconfig.json to that directory.
findings.evidence: Test output shows: FAIL terraform/.terraform/modules/codebuild/tests/products.test.ts - Error: Transform failed with 1 error: [TSCONFIG_ERROR] Failed to load tsconfig. Two test suites fail this way.
findings.impact: Tests appear to fail in CI/CD even though auth-service tests (83 tests) all pass. This masks the true test status and could hide real failures.

findings.title: Weak email validation regex allows invalid addresses
findings.description: Email validation uses a simple regex /^[^\s@]+@[^\s@]+\.[^\s@]+$/ that allows many invalid email formats such as 'user@domain.c', 'a@b.co..m', or 'user..name@domain.com'. RFC 5322 compliant email validation is complex, but this regex is too permissive.
findings.fix_suggestion: Use a more robust email regex or validate via RFC 5322 standards. A better regex: /^[^\s@]+@[^\s.@]([^@]*[^\s.@])?\.([a-zA-Z]{2,})+$/ or use a library like 'email-validator' or 'isemail'. At minimum, require valid TLD (>= 2 chars) and prevent consecutive dots.
findings.evidence: const emailRegex = /^[^\s@]+@[^\s@]+\.[^\s@]+$/; — This allows single-character TLDs, consecutive dots, and many other invalid formats.
findings.impact: Invalid email addresses can be registered in the system, leading to undeliverable verification emails and poor user experience. Database may accumulate garbage data.

findings.title: Hardcoded legacy password salt exposed in source code
findings.description: Line 8 contains LEGACY_PASSWORD_SALT = 'tnbm_salt_2024' as a hardcoded constant visible in the Git repository and code. While the system does migrate to per-user random salts, this legacy salt could be used to crack passwords if the codebase is compromised or leaked. This salt was likely from an older system.
findings.fix_suggestion: Move LEGACY_PASSWORD_SALT to AWS Secrets Manager (alongside JWT_SECRET and RESEND_API_KEY). Fetch it on cold start just like other secrets. This way the salt is not exposed in source code. Code change: Replace hardcoded constant with getenv or fetch from SecretsManager, similar to JWT secret handling.
findings.evidence: const LEGACY_PASSWORD_SALT = 'tnbm_salt_2024';
findings.impact: If this repository is ever breached or leaked, and if old password hashes are leaked separately, attackers have the salt needed to perform offline dictionary attacks on legacy passwords. Reduces password security for accounts that haven't been migrated.

findings.title: Unsafe JSON.parse of untrusted cursor data in admin list users
findings.description: Line 109 in admin.mjs decodes and parses a base64url-encoded cursor from the client without error handling. While the cursor is user-controlled DynamoDB LastEvaluatedKey data, a malformed cursor could crash the function or cause unexpected behavior.
findings.fix_suggestion: Wrap the JSON.parse in a try-catch block. On parse error, reset the cursor and start from the beginning or return an error: try { lastEvaluatedKey = JSON.parse(...); } catch { lastEvaluatedKey = undefined; return res(400, { error: 'Invalid cursor' }, origin); }
findings.evidence: let lastEvaluatedKey = cursor ? JSON.parse(Buffer.from(cursor, 'base64url').toString()) : undefined;
findings.impact: Malformed cursor input could cause the function to throw an unhandled error, returning 500 instead of handling gracefully. Potential DoS vector if invalid cursors are sent repeatedly.

findings.title: Admin allowlist JSON parsing without error handling
findings.description: Lines 23 and 30 in admin.mjs parse JSON from environment variable or Secrets Manager without try-catch. If the JSON is malformed, the function will throw and cause a runtime error during cold start or when the cache expires.
findings.fix_suggestion:
Wrap both JSON.parse calls in try-catch. On error, either return an empty allowlist (deny all admin access) or log a detailed error and skip caching. Example:
try { _adminAllowlist = JSON.parse(raw); } catch (e) { console.error('Invalid admin allowlist JSON:', e); _adminAllowlist = []; }
findings.evidence: _adminAllowlist = JSON.parse(raw); (line 23) and _adminAllowlist = JSON.parse(SecretString); (line 30) both lack error handling.
findings.impact: Malformed admin allowlist JSON (due to misconfiguration or Secrets Manager update) will crash the Lambda, making all admin endpoints unavailable. This could lock out administrators during an incident.

findings.title: Multiple SecretsManagerClient instances created on every secret fetch
findings.description: Lines 46, 60 in index.mjs and lines 28 in admin.mjs create new SecretsManagerClient() instances on every call. These should be created once and reused. Current code creates a new client for each secret fetch, even within the same cold start.
findings.fix_suggestion: Create a single SecretsManagerClient instance at module load time and reuse it: const smClient = new SecretsManagerClient({}); Then use smClient.send(...) in all functions. Same for DynamoDBClient (already done correctly).
findings.evidence: Inside getJwtSecret() and getResendApiKey(): const sm = new SecretsManagerClient({}); — Both create new instances rather than reusing a singleton.
findings.impact: Unnecessary resource allocation and connection overhead. While caching mitigates repeated calls within a cold start, the code is inefficient and could cause connection limit issues if secrets are fetched frequently.

findings.title: X-App-Origin header not validated against registered apps
findings.description: The X-App-Origin header is used to look up app branding but is not validated. The code silently falls back to default branding if the app is not found, but there is no validation that the origin is from an allowed list. This could allow arbitrary app names in logs and responses.
findings.fix_suggestion: Optionally: validate that the app origin is one of the known registered apps. This is already handled gracefully (falls back to default), so this is a nice-to-have rather than a critical fix.
findings.evidence: const appOrigin = event.headers?.['x-app-origin'] || ''; const app = APP_REGISTRY[appOrigin]; if (!app) { console.warn(...) } — Any string is accepted and logged.
findings.impact: Low risk, but unknown app origins can be logged with warnings and used in email branding. Could cause confusion in logs or be used to inject unexpected branding into user communications. Not a security vulnerability but a data quality concern.

lint.output: 46 linting issues found via ruff: 40 unused imports (fixable), 3 ambiguous variable names (E741), 3 f-strings without placeholders. Run 'ruff check --fix' to auto-fix.

findings.title: Shell command construction with unquoted file paths
findings.description: In regression.py:256, the pytest coverage command is constructed with an f-string that includes an unquoted file path: f"python3 -m pytest --cov=. --cov-report=json:{cov_json} --cov-report=term". If the path contains spaces or shell metacharacters, the command will fail or have unexpected behavior.
findings.fix_suggestion: Quote the path: use shlex.quote(cov_json) or f"python3 -m pytest --cov=. --cov-report=json:'{cov_json}' --cov-report=term"
findings.evidence: cov_cmd = f"python3 -m pytest --cov=. --cov-report=json:{cov_json} --cov-report=term"
findings.impact: Commands with paths containing spaces or special characters will break, potentially causing regression tests to fail with confusing error messages.

findings.title: Subprocess shell=True usage with command injection potential
findings.description: The run_cmd function in regression.py uses subprocess.run with shell=True. While the commands come from configuration (trusted input), using shell=True increases the attack surface and makes the code more fragile. Best practice is to avoid shell=True and pass arguments as a list.
findings.fix_suggestion: Consider redesigning to use subprocess.run with shlex.split() to parse the command string safely, or break commands into components. Document that test_command must be a valid shell command.
findings.evidence:
proc = subprocess.run(
    cmd,
    cwd=cwd,
    shell=True,
    capture_output=True,
    text=True,
    timeout=timeout_s,
    env={**os.environ},
)
findings.impact: Increased risk if configuration is ever loaded from untrusted sources. Current design mitigates this by using config-based commands, but the pattern is fragile.

findings.title: 46 unused imports and code quality issues from ruff linter
findings.description: Ruff linter found 46 issues: 40 unused imports (automatically fixable), 3 ambiguous variable names (E741 - using 'l' as a variable in lambdas/comprehensions), and 3 f-strings without placeholders. These should be fixed to maintain code quality and clarity.
findings.fix_suggestion: Run 'python3 -m ruff check --fix backoffice/ tests/' to automatically fix 40 issues. Manually rename 'l' to clearer names like 'lane' or 'item' to fix E741 warnings.
findings.evidence: Examples: backoffice/aggregate.py:615 imports normalize_finding but doesn't use it; tests/test_delivery.py:782 uses 'l' in lambda expression; tests/test_servers.py:48 has f-string without placeholders
findings.impact: Reduces code clarity, increases maintenance burden, and indicates incomplete refactoring in some areas.

findings.title: CORS header echoing with wildcard allowlist
findings.description: In api_server.py:184-187, if the allowed_origins list contains '*', the code will echo back the user-provided Origin header value instead of sending just '*'. This is incorrect CORS behavior.
findings.fix_suggestion: Fix the logic: if "*" in allowed: self.send_header("Access-Control-Allow-Origin", "*") elif origin in allowed: self.send_header("Access-Control-Allow-Origin", origin)
findings.evidence:
if "*" in allowed or origin in allowed:
    self.send_header("Access-Control-Allow-Origin", origin or "*")
findings.impact: Incorrect CORS behavior if wildcard is in allowlist. Current defaults (localhost only) are not affected, but future configuration changes could introduce this issue.

findings.title: Large file complexity: server.py (933 lines)
findings.description: The server.py file is the largest module at 933 lines, containing DashboardServer, APIHandler, and supporting functions. Large files become harder to understand, test, and maintain.
findings.fix_suggestion: Consider breaking into separate modules in a future refactoring: server_base.py (DashboardServer), api_handler.py (APIHandler), server_main.py (setup). This is not urgent but would improve maintainability.
findings.evidence: server.py is 933 lines total, containing multiple classes and concerns mixed together.
findings.impact: Harder to understand, test in isolation, and maintain. Changes to one concern may inadvertently affect another.

findings.title: Bare exception handler in try_read_json
findings.description: In regression.py:126, a bare 'except Exception:' catches all exceptions. While intentional for robustness, it silently swallows errors that might indicate real problems like MemoryError.
findings.fix_suggestion: Catch specific exceptions: 'except (json.JSONDecodeError, FileNotFoundError, OSError)' instead of bare Exception.
findings.evidence:
except Exception:
    return None
findings.impact: May hide unexpected errors. Callers won't know why a file couldn't be read.

findings.title: Missing validation of Content-Length header
findings.description: In api_server.py:213, the Content-Length header is converted to int without bounds checking. A large value could cause memory issues when reading the request body.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add bounds check: MAX_REQUEST_SIZE = 1024 * 1024; if length > MAX_REQUEST_SIZE: raise ValueError("Request too large")
findings.evidence: length = int(self.headers.get("Content-Length", 0))
findings.impact: Potential DoS via large Content-Length headers, though mitigated by HTTP server's built-in limits.

findings.title: Potential inefficiency in finding normalization
findings.description: The normalize_finding function is called for every finding in the backlog merge. While correct, the code could benefit from caching if the same finding is normalized multiple times.
findings.fix_suggestion: Monitor performance in production. If profiling shows this as a bottleneck, consider memoizing normalize_finding. Currently acceptable.
findings.evidence: Finding normalization is repeated in loops across multiple findings.
findings.impact: Minor performance impact on large portfolios. Not currently an issue but could become one as the system scales to thousands of findings.

findings.title: Inconsistent path handling: mixing os.path and pathlib.Path
findings.description: Several files mix os.path operations with string paths instead of consistently using pathlib.Path. While functional, this reduces consistency with modern Python best practices.
findings.fix_suggestion: Convert to pathlib: stdout_path = Path(out_dir) / f"{label}.stdout.log"
findings.evidence: stdout_path = os.path.join(out_dir, f"{label}.stdout.log")
findings.impact: Reduced code consistency and clarity.

lint.output: ESLint found 5 errors and 8 warnings. Errors: setState in effects (4 instances), unused variable CheckCircle2. Warnings: unused variables (SUPPORT_EMAIL, randomUUID, SRSRating, domainProgress, readiness, Filter, selectedTrack).

tests.output: Test suites: 2 failed (terraform modules with missing tsconfig), 10 passed. Tests: 14521 passed. TSCONFIG_ERROR: Failed to load tsconfig for terraform/.terraform/modules/codebuild/tests/*.test.ts

findings.title: setState called synchronously in useEffect hooks
findings.description: Four components violate React's hooks best practices by calling setState directly within effect bodies without dependency management. This causes cascading re-renders and performance degradation. Affected files: QuizPageClient.tsx (setElapsedSeconds), TutorPage.tsx (setSelectedTrackId x2), SaveFactButton.tsx (setSaved), StudyContext.tsx (loadData).
findings.fix_suggestion: Initialize state variable before the effect runs or use a separate effect for initialization. For timer case: call setElapsedSeconds(0) inside the interval callback or use a flag to track first render.
findings.impact: Performance regression: cascading renders reduce responsiveness. React DevTools will show warnings in production. User experience suffers during quiz/study sessions with visible lag.

findings.title: Unused imports and variables across codebase
findings.description: 8 unused variables/imports detected: SUPPORT_EMAIL (api/index.mjs:8), randomUUID (planner/index.mjs:5), SRSRating (srs-engine.test.ts:9), domainProgress (insights/page.tsx:72), readiness (insights/page.tsx:215), CheckCircle2 (QuizPageClient.tsx:57), Filter (tracks/page.tsx:8), selectedTrack (tutor/page.tsx:111). These increase bundle size and create code maintenance debt.
findings.fix_suggestion: Remove all unused imports and variable declarations. Use ESLint's --fix flag to auto-remove.
findings.evidence:
const SUPPORT_EMAIL = process.env.SUPPORT_EMAIL || 'hello@codyjo.com';
// SUPPORT_EMAIL is never referenced anywhere in the file
findings.impact: Code bloat, maintenance confusion, potential security risk if unused variables held sensitive data. ESLint warnings indicate code quality issues.

findings.title: Test infrastructure configuration error in Vitest
findings.description: Vitest is discovering test files in terraform/.terraform/modules/codebuild/tests/ which has no tsconfig.json, causing TSCONFIG_ERROR failures. The node_modules within terraform directory (1000+ files) should be excluded from test discovery.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add vitest exclude pattern to vitest.config.ts: exclude: ['**/node_modules/**', 'terraform/**', '.terraform/**']. Alternatively, move terraform module or add gitignore pattern.
findings.evidence:
Test Files: 2 failed | 10 passed
FAIL terraform/.terraform/modules/codebuild/tests/products.test.ts
FAIL terraform/.terraform/modules/codebuild/tests/verify-dist.test.ts
Error: Transform failed with 1 error: [TSCONFIG_ERROR] Failed to load tsconfig
findings.impact: CI/CD pipeline fails on test execution. Blocks merges and deployments. False negatives hide actual test failures.

findings.title: Missing error handling for decodeURIComponent in API routes
findings.description: The API handler uses decodeURIComponent() on path segments without try-catch, which can throw URIError if passed malformed UTF-8. This could crash the Lambda handler or expose stack traces. Affects: handleGetSharedProgress (line 1491), handleGetDomainProgress (line 1023, 1048), handleUpdateDomainProgress (line 1047, 1048).
findings.evidence:
const token = decodeURIComponent(segments[segments.length - 1]);
const shareResult = await ddb.send(new GetCommand({...}));
findings.impact: Malformed URLs can crash the API handler, causing 500 errors and service degradation. Potential DoS vector with specially crafted URLs. Error stack traces may leak internal paths.

findings.title: Promise returned but not awaited in async function
findings.description: In LoginPageClient.tsx line 68, setupEncryption(password) is called without await inside a try-catch. The catch block won't catch errors from setupEncryption because the promise is unhandled. This masks potential failures in encryption setup.
findings.fix_suggestion:
Change to: try { await setupEncryption(password); } catch (e) { console.warn('Encryption setup failed:', e); }
This ensures the await completes and logs the error.
findings.impact: Encryption setup failures are silently ignored. User data may not be encrypted properly. Silent failures reduce debuggability.

findings.title: Plausible analytics script uses dangerouslySetInnerHTML
findings.description: The Plausible analytics initialization script in layout.tsx:15 uses dangerouslySetInnerHTML with a hardcoded string. While this specific string is safe (it's hardcoded), the pattern should be avoided for future maintenance and security posture.
findings.fix_suggestion: Replace with a Next.js Script component with strategy='afterInteractive' and remove dangerouslySetInnerHTML. Or load Plausible via standard async script tag (line 14 already does this correctly).
findings.evidence: <script dangerouslySetInnerHTML={{ __html: 'window.plausible=window.plausible||function(){(plausible.q=plausible.q||[]).push(arguments)},plausible.init=plausible.init||function(i){plausible.o=i||{}};plausible.init()' }} />
findings.impact: Low risk for this hardcoded case, but sets a dangerous precedent. If this pattern is used elsewhere with dynamic content, could lead to XSS.

findings.title: Multiple token lookups and cryptographic operations in auth-context
findings.description: auth-context.tsx caches encryption keys in localStorage and decrypts them on every auth operation. The getCachedEncryptionMaterial() function is called multiple times per render cycle, and JSON.parse errors are caught silently, masking corruption issues.
findings.fix_suggestion: 1. Memoize getCachedEncryptionMaterial() result. 2. Log JSON.parse errors for debugging: catch (e) { console.warn('Corrupted encryption cache:', e); return null; }
findings.impact: Performance: repeated localStorage access and JSON parsing. Correctness: silent catch blocks mask data corruption. User may not realize encryption cache is corrupted.

findings.title: Missing null checks in JWT verification
findings.description: In lambda/api/index.mjs, the verifyToken function at line 153 splits the token and accesses elements without bounds checking. If a malformed token has fewer than 3 parts, accessing [2] succeeds but could be undefined, passing to timingSafeEqual.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add validation: const parts = token.split('.'); if (parts.length !== 3) return null; const [header, body, signature] = parts;
findings.impact: Malformed JWT tokens may not be properly rejected. Potential for token validation bypass if signature is undefined.

findings.title: Error handling gap in parseJsonBody function
findings.description: The parseJsonBody function in lambda/api/index.mjs and lambda/tutor/index.mjs differs slightly between implementations. One allows empty bodies (tutor) while api requires them, leading to inconsistency.
findings.fix_suggestion: Standardize parseJsonBody implementation across all lambdas (api, tutor, planner). Create a shared utility or document the intentional differences.
findings.impact: Inconsistency between lambdas could lead to different behavior on same endpoint if deployed across services. Maintenance confusion.

findings.title: No pagination on flashcard SRS state queries
findings.description: The handleGetFlashcardStates function in lambda/api/index.mjs queries all flashcard states for a user without pagination limits. For users with 10,000+ cards, this could cause memory issues and slow responses.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add Limit: 100 (or suitable batch size) and implement pagination with LastEvaluatedKey. Return pageToken in response for client pagination.
findings.impact: Large datasets cause high latency and memory consumption. Risk of timeout on Lambda. Potential DoS if user enrolls in many tracks.

findings.title: Build verification failure: forbidden 'Back Office' references in homepage
findings.description: The npm run verify:dist command fails because the built homepage HTML contains forbidden references to 'Back Office' that should have been removed according to the HANDOFF.md specification. The specification explicitly states that 'Back Office public pages, docs, admin entry points, and demo routes' were removed from the public site. The verify-dist script checks the dist/index.html output and fails when these references are found, indicating incomplete cleanup of Back Office branding from the public interface.
findings.fix_suggestion: Remove the Back Office link from the nav dropdown menu in src/components/Nav.astro (line 31) and remove the Back Office project card from src/pages/index.astro (line 255). Both references should be completely deleted from the source, not just disabled or hidden. The verify-dist script enforces that 'Back Office' and '/back-office' strings do not appear anywhere in the built dist/index.html output.
findings.evidence:
Back Office Also found in src/pages/index.astro at line 255 in the projects section:
07

Back Office
findings.impact: The CI/CD pipeline fails at the npm run verify:dist stage, preventing deployments. The build verification is a critical check (buildspec-ci.yml line 14) that ensures the public site does not expose Back Office branding, and its failure blocks the entire pipeline.

tests.output: 3 tests failing in src/__tests__/auth-theme-context.test.tsx due to localStorage key mismatches

findings.title: Test failures due to localStorage key mismatch
findings.description: Three tests in auth-theme-context.test.tsx are failing because the test code uses legacy localStorage keys that don't match the current implementation. The theme context code uses 'cordivent_theme' but the test checks for 'bible_theme'. The auth context code uses 'cordivent_token' and 'cordivent_enc_cache' but the tests check for 'portis_token' and 'portis_enc_cache'. These are remnants of a past refactoring where the localStorage keys were renamed from 'portis_*' to 'cordivent_*' in the implementation but not in the tests.
findings.fix_suggestion: Update the test to use the current localStorage keys: Change 'bible_theme' to 'cordivent_theme', 'portis_token' to 'cordivent_token', and 'portis_enc_cache' to 'cordivent_enc_cache' in the test assertions.
findings.evidence:
Test line 127: expect(localStorage.getItem('bible_theme')).toBe('light'); // but code uses 'cordivent_theme'
Test line 175: expect(localStorage.getItem('portis_token')).toBe('token-1'); // but code uses 'cordivent_token'
Test line 207: expect(localStorage.getItem('portis_enc_cache')).toContain('enc-master'); // but code uses 'cordivent_enc_cache'
findings.impact: Tests are failing in CI/CD pipeline, blocking merges. This prevents validation that localStorage persistence is working correctly. The test suite cannot pass until these keys are synchronized with the implementation.

findings.title: Storage key inconsistency between auth modules
findings.description: The coordinator-api.ts file uses a legacy localStorage key 'portis_token' on line 3, while the auth-context.tsx uses the current key 'cordivent_token' on line 18. This inconsistency means that coordinator API calls may fail to retrieve authentication tokens because they're looking for a different key than where the auth context is storing them.
findings.fix_suggestion: Update coordinator-api.ts line 3 to use the same key as auth-context.tsx: const TOKEN_KEY = 'cordivent_token';
findings.evidence:
coordinator-api.ts line 3: const TOKEN_KEY = 'portis_token';
auth-context.tsx line 18: const TOKEN_KEY = 'cordivent_token';
findings.impact: Coordinator API calls may fail to authenticate because the getToken() function in coordinator-api.ts retrieves from the wrong localStorage key. This could cause API failures for coordinator/organizer operations.

findings.title: TypeScript any type used in test file
findings.description: The terraform test file (terraform/.terraform/modules/codebuild/tests/verify-dist.test.ts) contains 4 instances of explicit 'any' type usage, which violates the ESLint rule @typescript-eslint/no-explicit-any. This bypasses type safety and makes the code more fragile.
findings.fix_suggestion: Replace 'as any' with proper TypeScript type definitions. Create a type interface that matches the expected structure: interface FileReader { fileExists(path: string): boolean; readFile(path: string): string; } Then use: fileExists as FileReader['fileExists']
findings.evidence:
Line 36: fileExists: fileExists as any,
Line 37: readFile: readFile as any,
Line 56: fileExists: fileExists as any,
Line 57: readFile: readFile as any,
findings.impact: ESLint errors prevent the build from passing. Type safety is compromised, making refactoring and debugging more difficult.

findings.title: Image optimization warning in contact page
findings.description: The contact page (src/app/contact/page.tsx) uses a native tag instead of Next.js Image component. While not an error, ESLint warns that this bypasses Next.js image optimization and can result in slower LCP (Largest Contentful Paint) and higher bandwidth usage.
findings.fix_suggestion: Replace with Next.js Image component: import Image from 'next/image'; Then use:
findings.evidence: Contact page uses tag instead of from 'next/image'
findings.impact: Suboptimal image performance on the contact page. The image may load slower and consume more bandwidth than necessary, impacting Core Web Vitals and user experience.

findings.title: Missing input validation on password reset endpoint
findings.description: The reset-password page (src/app/reset-password/page.tsx) validates password length (minimum 10 characters) client-side but the corresponding API endpoint should also validate this server-side. Missing server-side validation could allow attackers to bypass client-side checks and submit weak passwords.
findings.fix_suggestion: Implement server-side password validation in the reset password API endpoint to enforce a minimum of 10 characters and any other password complexity requirements. Never rely solely on client-side validation.
findings.evidence: Client-side validation exists: 'Password must be at least 10 characters' error. However, no evidence that the backend API enforces the same validation.
findings.impact: Security vulnerability: weak passwords could be set if an attacker bypasses the client-side validation. Password policies should be enforced server-side, not just client-side.

findings.title: Duplicate API URL retrieval pattern
findings.description: The code pattern 'process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_API_URL || ""' is duplicated across 20+ files. This creates maintenance burden and inconsistent error handling. Some files default to empty string, which could cause silent failures if the environment variable is not set.
findings.fix_suggestion: Extract the API URL retrieval into a single utility function in src/lib/api-config.ts and import it everywhere. Consider throwing an error during build if the required environment variable is not set.
findings.evidence: Multiple occurrences of: const apiUrl = process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_API_URL || ''; found in login, settings, register, admin pages and content components.
findings.impact: Code duplication. Inconsistent default behavior - if the API URL is not configured, many operations will fail silently with a 404 to an empty URL. Harder to maintain and update centrally.

findings.title: Insufficient validation on DynamoDB expression attribute names in UpdateExpression
findings.description: In lambda/api/index.mjs around line 1701, attribute names are sanitized for DynamoDB UpdateExpression by replacing non-alphanumeric characters with underscores. This approach, while preventing injection, is fragile. A safer approach would be to use explicit whitelisting or schema validation.
findings.evidence:
const safeKey = key.replace(/[^a-zA-Z0-9]/g, '_');
expressionParts.push(`#${safeKey} = :${safeKey}`);
findings.impact: While DynamoDB injection is mitigated, overly permissive input handling could lead to silent data corruption or unintended field updates

findings.title: Multiple sequential DynamoDB queries could be batched for improved latency
findings.description: In handleGetStats (lambda/api/index.mjs around line 1730), three GetCommand calls could be combined into one BatchGetCommand to reduce round trips.
findings.fix_suggestion: Use BatchGetCommand for the GetCommand calls to reduce 3 calls to 1
findings.evidence: const [foodResult, waterResult, medResult, wellnessResult, goalsResult] = await Promise.all([...multiple GetCommand calls...]);
findings.impact: Higher latency for stats endpoint, increased DynamoDB call costs

findings.title: Weak random number generation for verification codes
findings.description: Email verification codes and password reset codes are generated using Math.random() instead of cryptographically secure random number generation. This reduces the entropy of these security-critical codes from 64 to approximately 53 bits, making them susceptible to brute force attacks if an attacker can guess the timing of generation.
findings.fix_suggestion: Replace Math.random() with crypto.getRandomValues() to generate cryptographically secure verification codes: const bytes = randomBytes(3); const code = String(parseInt(bytes.toString('hex').slice(0, 6), 16) % 1000000).padStart(6, '0');
findings.impact: An attacker could more easily brute force email verification codes or password reset codes, especially if they can narrow down the time window of generation. While rate limiting provides some protection, the weak randomness is a vulnerability.

findings.title: Payload size validation not applied to all endpoints
findings.description: Size validation (100KB limit) is only applied to the daily verse save endpoint. Other endpoints like journals, studies, and bookmarks that accept JSON payloads do not have explicit size limits, which could allow unbounded data submissions.
findings.fix_suggestion: Apply consistent payload size validation to all endpoints that accept POST/PUT requests. Create a helper function like validatePayloadSize(event, maxBytes) and call it at the start of all data submission handlers.
findings.impact: Users could potentially submit very large payloads to other endpoints, consuming database storage, bandwidth, and Lambda memory. While rate limiting and other controls exist, this creates an inconsistency in protection.

findings.title: Unused eslint-disable directive
findings.description: A coverage report file contains an unused eslint-disable directive, indicating either overly broad linting rules or stale configuration.
findings.fix_suggestion: Remove the eslint-disable directive from coverage/lcov-report/block-navigation.js or add it to eslint ignore patterns. Coverage reports should typically be excluded from linting.
findings.evidence: Unused eslint-disable directive (no problems were reported)
findings.impact: Minor - this is in a generated coverage report and doesn't affect production code, but indicates linting configuration could be refined.

findings.title: Route matching uses path.endsWith() which could cause false positives
findings.description: API route matching in the handler uses path.endsWith() for route detection (e.g., path.endsWith('/api/studies')). This approach could incorrectly match unintended paths like '/some-other-api/studies' or '/prefix/api/studies'.
findings.fix_suggestion: Use exact path matching: const pathMatch = (route) => path === route || path.endsWith(route + '/'); Or better, use a proper router. Alternatively, use path === route for exact matches since API Gateway should handle path normalization.
findings.evidence: if (path.endsWith('/api/studies') && method === 'POST') return await handleSaveStudy(event, user, origin);
findings.impact: While current API Gateway routing likely prevents this, it's a code smell that could lead to issues if the routing layer changes or if there are misconfigured paths. Could inadvertently route requests to unintended handlers.

findings.title: Limited error context in API error responses
findings.description: The main error handler catches all exceptions and returns a generic '500 Internal server error' message. While this prevents information leakage, it makes debugging more difficult. Production logging relies only on console.error().
findings.fix_suggestion: Add structured logging with correlation IDs: const requestId = event.requestContext?.requestId || randomUUID(); Log errors with context: { requestId, userId, endpoint, error, stack }. Consider using a structured logging library.
findings.impact: Makes production debugging harder. CloudWatch logs exist but aren't easily queried. No structured logging with correlation IDs or request tracing. Should not expose detailed errors to clients but internal monitoring could be improved.

lint.output: 3 errors in src/app/layout.tsx, 4 errors in terraform tests, multiple warnings for unused variables and missing dependencies

findings.title: Using <a> instead of <Link /> in layout.tsx
findings.description: src/app/layout.tsx lines 99-101 use standard <a> elements for navigation to internal routes instead of Next.js <Link /> components. This bypasses client-side routing and causes full page reloads, degrading performance and UX.
findings.fix_suggestion: Replace with Next.js Link component: import Link from 'next/link'; then use <Link href="/cards">Card Meanings</Link>
findings.evidence: Card Meanings Spread Guides Blog
findings.impact: Full page reloads on navigation to card library, guides, and blog. Loses application state, re-fetches resources, poor perceived performance.

findings.title: Dependency vulnerability: fast-xml-parser entity expansion bypass
findings.description: fast-xml-parser 4.0.0-beta.3 through 5.5.6 has a moderate severity vulnerability (GHSA-jp2q-39xq-3w4g) where entity expansion limits can be bypassed due to JavaScript falsy evaluation. This is a transitive dependency via @aws-sdk/xml-builder.
findings.fix_suggestion: Run `npm audit fix` to upgrade to patched version. aws-sdk will be updated to use safe XML parser version.
findings.evidence: npm audit: fast-xml-parser 4.0.0-beta.3 - 5.5.6, Severity: moderate, @aws-sdk/xml-builder 3.894.0 - 3.972.14 -> Depends on vulnerable versions
findings.impact: Potential XXE (XML External Entity) attack surface through AWS SDK XML parsing. Could lead to information disclosure or DoS.

findings.title: Multiple dependency vulnerabilities in Next.js 16
findings.description: Next.js 16.0.0-beta.0 to 16.1.6 has 5 moderate severity vulnerabilities: HTTP request smuggling, unbounded image cache growth, DoS from buffering, CSRF bypasses. Fix available via npm audit fix --force (next@16.2.1).
findings.fix_suggestion: Run `npm audit fix --force` to upgrade to Next.js 16.2.1 or later. Review and test thoroughly as this upgrades from beta to stable.
findings.evidence: npm audit: next 16.0.0-beta.0 - 16.1.6, Severity: moderate (5 vulnerabilities), Fix available via npm audit fix --force
findings.impact: Request smuggling could bypass security controls. Disk cache exhaustion on production OG endpoint. CSRF bypass in admin. DoS risk.

findings.title: Missing alt text on elements in journal entry page
findings.description: src/app/journal/entry/page.tsx lines 424, 442, 445 have elements without meaningful alt attributes. WCAG 2.1 AA requires all images to have alt text.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add meaningful alt text: alt="Journal entry image" or alt="User uploaded content". Use alt="" only for purely decorative images.
findings.evidence: Line 424-445: elements with missing or empty alt attributes for user-uploaded journal images
findings.impact: Screen readers cannot describe uploaded journal images. Accessibility violation for visually impaired users.

findings.title: Using instead of next/image for journal images
findings.description: src/app/journal/entry/page.tsx line 442 and src/app/journal/view/page.tsx line 388 use instead of Next.js component. Bypasses automatic optimization, format conversion, responsive sizing.
findings.fix_suggestion: Replace with: import Image from 'next/image'; <Image src={imageUrls[key]} alt="..." width={300} height={128} />. Requires valid URL.
findings.evidence: using plain img tag, not Image component
findings.impact: Slower page loads, larger bandwidth, higher LCP (Largest Contentful Paint), poor Core Web Vitals scores.

findings.title: Unused variable in admin test: _origin

findings.title: React Hook missing dependency: masterKey in history page
findings.description: src/app/history/page.tsx line 95 useEffect uses 'masterKey' but doesn't include it in dependency array. Causes stale closure when key changes.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add 'masterKey' to dependency array: useEffect(() => {...}, [masterKey])
findings.evidence: useEffect(() => { ...uses masterKey... }, []) // missing masterKey dependency
findings.impact: If encryption masterKey is updated (key recovery), effect won't re-run, showing data decrypted with old key.

findings.title: React Hook missing dependency: refreshSavedReadings in reading page
findings.description: src/app/reading/page.tsx line 525 useEffect calls 'refreshSavedReadings' but doesn't include it in dependency array.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add 'refreshSavedReadings' to deps or wrap with useCallback: useEffect(() => {...}, [refreshSavedReadings])
findings.evidence: useEffect(() => { refreshSavedReadings(); }, []) // missing refreshSavedReadings dependency
findings.impact: Effect won't re-run if function changes. May show stale reading data after creating new readings.

findings.title: Unused imports in learn page: useEffect, AuthGuard
